meta.originalTitle: The Origin of Paper 造纸术起源

children.text: You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 1-13, which are based on Reading Passage 1.

The word paper derives from the Greek term for the ancient Egyptian writing material called papyrus. In about 2400 BC, the Egyptians discovered how to make a writing surface out of papyrus, a type of reed that grows along waterways in southern Europe and North Africa. The Egyptians cut the plant into strips which they softened in water. Papyrus was cross-woven into a mat and then pounded into a hard, thin sheet.

As the papyrus plant requires subtropical conditions to grow, papyrus was not much used in Europe at that time; instead, the main material used for writing was parchment. This was made from animal skin and was extremely expensive. In fact, it has been estimated that a single book written on parchment required the skins of 300 sheep. The skins had to be specially prepared by drying them and they were then stretched on a special frame. It is not known when parchment was first introduced, but it was the main writing material in Europe for hundreds of years.

Paper—made from pulp, rags and fibres of plants—seems to have been invented in China and is considered to be one of the Four Great Inventions of Ancient China. In 105 AD, under the Han-Dynasty emperor Ho-Ti, a government official in China named Ts'ai Lun was the first to start a papermaking industry. Ts'ai Lun seems to have made his paper by mixing finely chopped mulberry bark and hemp rags with water, mashing the mixture flat with a stone mortar, and then pressing out the water and letting it dry in the sun. He may have based his idea on bark cloth, which was very common in China and also made from mulberry.

Previously, during the Shang (1600-1050 BC) and Zhou (1050-256 BC) dynasties of Ancient China, documents were ordinarily written on bone or bamboo (on tablets or bamboo strips bound and rolled together into scrolls), making them very heavy and awkward to transport. The light material of silk was sometimes used, but was normally too expensive to consider. When paper was first invented, it was used for purposes of wrapping or padding protection for delicate bronze mirrors. Although paper for writing became popular by the 3rd century AD, it continued to be used for wrapping and other purposes.

During the Tang Dynasty (618-907 AD) paper was folded and sewn into square bags to preserve the flavour of tea. During the same period, according to a written account, tea was served from baskets with multi-coloured paper cups and paper napkins of different sizes and shapes. During the Chinese Song Dynasty (960-1279 AD) not only did the government produce the world's first known printed paper money, or banknote, but paper money bestowed as gifts was wrapped in special paper envelopes.

Paper spread widely outside China; other Asian cultures, even after seeing paper, could not make it themselves. Instruction in the manufacturing process was required, and the Chinese were reluctant to share their secrets. It made its true push westward in 751 AD when the Tang Dynasty was at war with the Islamic world. During a battle on the banks of the Talas River, a Chinese caravan was captured which happened to include several papermakers. They were taken away to Samarkand, which was a good place to make paper because it had an abundant supply of hemp and flax. Samarkanders changed the technology of manufacturing paper. They began to prepare it in stone mills and Samarkand became an important papermaking centre.

The rudimentary and laborious process of papermaking was refined, and bulk manufacturing of paper began in Iran, where they invented a method to make a thicker sheet of paper, which helped transform papermaking from an art into an important business.

Gradually papermakers made their way further west through the Arab world—to Baghdad, Damascus and Cairo. Finally, when the Moors from North Africa invaded Spain and Portugal, they brought the technology with them and so it was that papermaking entered Europe in the 12th century.

In Europe, the preferred medium for the artists and literati of the time was still the smooth and lustrous parchment. The notion of paper being used as a practical everyday item did not occur until the 15th century, when Johannes Gutenberg perfected movable-type printing, which included the use of metal moulds and alloys, a special press, and oil-based inks, allowing the mass production of printed books. The birth of the modern paper and printing industry is commonly marked from this date.

Printing technology rapidly developed and created an ever-increasing demand for paper. The early European papers were made from recycled cotton and linen—and a huge trade quickly developed around the trading of old rags. It is said that the disease known as the Black Death entered England from Europe on these old rags. Yet soon this source became insufficient and some curious attempts were made to find new materials. Experiments with fibres such as straw, cabbage, wasp nests and finally wood resulted in inexpensive—and replaceable—materials for papermaking. Today, the long, soft fibres of softwoods such as spruce have become the most suitable source of pulp for the mass production of paper.
Do the following statements agree with the information given in Reading Passage 1?
In boxes 1–7 on your answer sheet, write:
if the statement agrees with the information
if the statement contradicts the information
if there is no information on this
In Ancient China, writing was occasionally done on silk.
Coloured paper was invented during the Tang Dynasty.
Papermakers from Samarkand were captured by the Chinese.
Papermaking as a large-scale industry originated in Iran.
Papermaking skills were brought to Europe via North Africa.
When Gutenberg invented movable type, paper materials were very expensive.
The end of the trade in rags was brought about by the difficulty of transporting them.

explanationSection.text:
题目翻译：中国古代偶尔在丝绸上书写。
答案：TRUE（正确）
解析：定位 Paragraph 4 中 “The light material of silk was sometimes used, but was normally too expensive to consider”，“sometimes used” 对应 “occasionally done”，明确提到古代中国偶尔用丝绸书写，因此判定为 TRUE。
题目翻译：彩色纸发明于唐朝。
答案：NOT GIVEN（未提及）
解析：定位 Paragraph 5 中 “tea was served from baskets with multi-coloured paper cups and paper napkins”，仅提到唐朝有 “彩色纸杯和纸巾”，未提及 “彩色纸的发明时间”，无相关信息，因此判定为 NOT GIVEN。
题目翻译：撒马尔罕的造纸匠被中国人俘虏。
答案：FALSE（错误）
解析：定位 Paragraph 6 中 “a Chinese caravan was captured which happened to include several papermakers. They were taken away to Samarkand”，明确提到 “中国造纸匠被俘虏并带至撒马尔罕”，与题干 “撒马尔罕造纸匠被中国人俘虏” 描述矛盾，因此判定为 FALSE。
题目翻译：造纸业作为大规模产业起源于伊朗。
答案：TRUE（正确）
解析：定位 Paragraph 7 中 “bulk manufacturing of paper began in Iran, where they invented a method to make a thicker sheet of paper, which helped transform papermaking from an art into an important business”，“bulk manufacturing” 对应 “large-scale industry”，“began” 对应 “originated”，明确提到伊朗开始大规模造纸，因此判定为 TRUE。
题目翻译：造纸技术经北非传入欧洲。
答案：TRUE（正确）
解析：定位 Paragraph 8 中 “when the Moors from North Africa invaded Spain and Portugal, they brought the technology with them and so it was that papermaking entered Europe”，明确提到北非摩尔人将造纸技术带入欧洲，因此判定为 TRUE。
题目翻译：谷登堡发明活字印刷术时，造纸原料非常昂贵。
答案：NOT GIVEN（未提及）
解析：Paragraph 9 仅提到谷登堡发明活字印刷术后，纸成为 “日常实用品”，未提及 “当时造纸原料的价格”，无相关信息，因此判定为 NOT GIVEN。
题目翻译：旧布贸易的终结是由于运输困难。
答案：FALSE（错误）
解析：定位 Paragraph 10 中 “soon this source became insufficient and some curious attempts were made to find new materials”，明确提到旧布贸易终结是因 “原料不足”，而非 “运输困难”，与题干描述矛盾，因此判定为 FALSE。

items.text:
（1）题目 1：In Ancient China, writing was occasionally done on silk.
题目翻译：中国古代偶尔在丝绸上书写。
答案：TRUE（正确）
解析：定位 Paragraph 4 中 “The light material of silk was sometimes used, but was normally too expensive to consider”，“sometimes used” 对应 “occasionally done”，明确提到古代中国偶尔用丝绸书写，因此判定为 TRUE。

（2）题目 2：Coloured paper was invented during the Tang Dynasty.
题目翻译：彩色纸发明于唐朝。
答案：NOT GIVEN（未提及）
解析：定位 Paragraph 5 中 “tea was served from baskets with multi-coloured paper cups and paper napkins”，仅提到唐朝有 “彩色纸杯和纸巾”，未提及 “彩色纸的发明时间”，无相关信息，因此判定为 NOT GIVEN。

（3）题目 3：Papermakers from Samarkand were captured by the Chinese.
题目翻译：撒马尔罕的造纸匠被中国人俘虏。
答案：FALSE（错误）
解析：定位 Paragraph 6 中 “a Chinese caravan was captured which happened to include several papermakers. They were taken away to Samarkand”，明确提到 “中国造纸匠被俘虏并带至撒马尔罕”，与题干 “撒马尔罕造纸匠被中国人俘虏” 描述矛盾，因此判定为 FALSE。

（4）题目 4：Papermaking as a large-scale industry originated in Iran.
题目翻译：造纸业作为大规模产业起源于伊朗。
答案：TRUE（正确）
解析：定位 Paragraph 7 中 “bulk manufacturing of paper began in Iran, where they invented a method to make a thicker sheet of paper, which helped transform papermaking from an art into an important business”，“bulk manufacturing” 对应 “large-scale industry”，“began” 对应 “originated”，明确提到伊朗开始大规模造纸，因此判定为 TRUE。

（5）题目 5：Papermaking skills were brought to Europe via North Africa.
题目翻译：造纸技术经北非传入欧洲。
答案：TRUE（正确）
解析：定位 Paragraph 8 中 “when the Moors from North Africa invaded Spain and Portugal, they brought the technology with them and so it was that papermaking entered Europe”，明确提到北非摩尔人将造纸技术带入欧洲，因此判定为 TRUE。

（6）题目 6：When Gutenberg invented movable type, paper materials were very expensive.
题目翻译：谷登堡发明活字印刷术时，造纸原料非常昂贵。
答案：NOT GIVEN（未提及）
解析：Paragraph 9 仅提到谷登堡发明活字印刷术后，纸成为 “日常实用品”，未提及 “当时造纸原料的价格”，无相关信息，因此判定为 NOT GIVEN。

（7）题目 7：The end of the trade in rags was brought about by the difficulty of transporting them.
题目翻译：旧布贸易的终结是由于运输困难。
答案：FALSE（错误）
解析：定位 Paragraph 10 中 “soon this source became insufficient and some curious attempts were made to find new materials”，明确提到旧布贸易终结是因 “原料不足”，而非 “运输困难”，与题干描述矛盾，因此判定为 FALSE。

children.text: from the passage for each answer.
of the plant were softened in water, woven and pounded
parchment made from the skin of animals such as
of a mulberry tree and hemp rags
ingredients were mixed with water, pressed and put outside in the sun

explanationSection.text:
题目翻译：埃及 - 制作方法 - 植物的_\___\__在水中软化、编织并捶打
答案：strips（条）
解析：定位 Paragraph 1 中 “The Egyptians cut the plant into strips which they softened in water. Papyrus was crosswoven into a mat and then pounded into a hard, thin sheet”，明确提到将植物切成 “条（strips）” 处理，因此答案为 “strips”。
题目翻译：欧洲 - 原料 - 用_\___\__等动物皮制成的羊皮纸
答案：sheep（羊）
解析：定位 Paragraph 2 中 “it has been estimated that a single book written on parchment required the skins of 300 sheep”，明确提到羊皮纸常用 “羊皮”，因此答案为 “sheep”。
题目翻译：欧洲 - 制作方法 - 兽皮经干燥后_\___\__
答案：stretched（拉伸）
解析：定位 Paragraph 2 中 “The skins had to be specially prepared by drying them and they were then stretched on a special frame”，明确提到兽皮干燥后 “拉伸”，因此答案为 “stretched”。
题目翻译：中国 - 原料 - 桑树的_\___\__和麻纤维
答案：bark（树皮）
解析：定位 Paragraph 3 中 “Ts’ai Lun seems to have made his paper by mixing finely chopped mulberry bark and hemp rags with water”，明确提到原料包括 “桑树皮”，因此答案为 “bark”。
题目翻译：\___\___ - 传入时间 - 公元 751 年 - 原料 - 亚麻和大麻
答案：Samarkand（撒马尔罕）
解析：定位 Paragraph 6 中 “in 751 AD... They were taken away to Samarkand, which was a good place to make paper because it had an abundant supply of hemp and flax”，明确提到公元 751 年造纸技术传入 “撒马尔罕”，因此答案为 “Samarkand”。
题目翻译：撒马尔罕 - 制作方法 - 使用_\___\__制成的磨具
答案：stone（石头）
解析：定位 Paragraph 6 中 “They began to prepare it in stone mills”，明确提到撒马尔罕用 “石磨” 加工原料，因此答案为 “stone”。

items.text:
（1）题目 8：Egypt - Method of production - \___\___ of the plant were softened in water, woven and pounded
题目翻译：埃及 - 制作方法 - 植物的_\___\__在水中软化、编织并捶打
答案：strips（条）
解析：定位 Paragraph 1 中 “The Egyptians cut the plant into strips which they softened in water. Papyrus was crosswoven into a mat and then pounded into a hard, thin sheet”，明确提到将植物切成 “条（strips）” 处理，因此答案为 “strips”。

（2）题目 9：Europe - Materials used - parchment made from the skin of animals such as \___\___
题目翻译：欧洲 - 原料 - 用_\___\__等动物皮制成的羊皮纸
答案：sheep（羊）
解析：定位 Paragraph 2 中 “it has been estimated that a single book written on parchment required the skins of 300 sheep”，明确提到羊皮纸常用 “羊皮”，因此答案为 “sheep”。

（3）题目 10：Europe - Method of production - skin was dried and \___\___
题目翻译：欧洲 - 制作方法 - 兽皮经干燥后_\___\__
答案：stretched（拉伸）
解析：定位 Paragraph 2 中 “The skins had to be specially prepared by drying them and they were then stretched on a special frame”，明确提到兽皮干燥后 “拉伸”，因此答案为 “stretched”。

（4）题目 11：China - Materials used - \___\___ of a mulberry tree and hemp rags
题目翻译：中国 - 原料 - 桑树的_\___\__和麻纤维
答案：bark（树皮）
解析：定位 Paragraph 3 中 “Ts’ai Lun seems to have made his paper by mixing finely chopped mulberry bark and hemp rags with water”，明确提到原料包括 “桑树皮”，因此答案为 “bark”。

（5）题目 12：\___\___ - Date of introduction - 751 AD - Materials used - flax and hemp
题目翻译：\___\___ - 传入时间 - 公元 751 年 - 原料 - 亚麻和大麻
答案：Samarkand（撒马尔罕）
解析：定位 Paragraph 6 中 “in 751 AD... They were taken away to Samarkand, which was a good place to make paper because it had an abundant supply of hemp and flax”，明确提到公元 751 年造纸技术传入 “撒马尔罕”，因此答案为 “Samarkand”。

（6）题目 13：Samarkand - Method of production - mills made of \___\___ were used
题目翻译：撒马尔罕 - 制作方法 - 使用_\___\__制成的磨具
答案：stone（石头）
解析：定位 Paragraph 6 中 “They began to prepare it in stone mills”，明确提到撒马尔罕用 “石磨” 加工原料，因此答案为 “stone”。
